summary: I would categorize myself as a gnostic because I don't have a strong religious background. Although I grew up going to church and even attended a Catholic college, I personally don't identify with organized religion. I respect religion, but my spiritual perspective aligns more with gnosticism.

I attended a Catholic college and grew up going to church, so I have a respect for the Catholic Church and religion in general. However, I don't personally identify with the Catholic Church or organized religion. My connection to it is more from my upbringing and education rather than personal faith.

I've worked at Harborview Medical Center since 2007, starting as a registered nurse in trauma surgical acute care and later moving to the trauma surgical intensive care units. Harborview has been my first and only nursing job since I got my license. During the COVID-19 pandemic, Harborview converted units to COVID ICUs where I worked extensively, especially as an ECMO specialist. The hospital's response to PPE and safety has been better than many others, partly due to our union's active involvement.

I currently live in Seattle, Washington, specifically on North Beacon Hill. Seattle is where I work and live, and it's also the city where Harborview Medical Center is located. My community and professional life are deeply rooted in Seattle.

I live and work in Washington state, specifically in Seattle. Harborview Medical Center serves as the only level one trauma center in the four-state area including Washington, Alaska, Idaho, and Montana. My nursing career and union activities have all taken place within Washington.
cues: level one trauma center for four states

summary: I live in North Beacon Hill, a neighborhood in Seattle, Washington. This is where I call home while I work nights at Harborview Medical Center. Living here places me close to my workplace and community.

I am an ECMO specialist, which means I help run the extracorporeal membrane oxygenation machine, an advanced life support system that can perform lung or heart-lung bypass. This role has been especially critical during the COVID-19 pandemic when we saw much higher volumes of patients needing this therapy. ECMO requires constant monitoring during a 12-hour shift, and it’s one of the most intense therapies we offer.

I have been a member of SEIU 1199 since I started at Harborview in 2007, though I became more active about two years ago. I serve as a union delegate and have taken on roles such as interim co-chair for the nurse staffing committee and chairing joint labor management meetings. During the COVID-19 pandemic, our union has been very active in bargaining over PPE, furloughs, and redeployment, which has helped ensure we have proper protections and a voice in hospital policies.

The COVID-19 pandemic drastically changed my work at Harborview, where I shifted mostly to COVID intensive care units that didn't exist before. The pandemic brought intense challenges like wearing full PPE, managing critically ill patients on ECMO, and dealing with mental and emotional exhaustion. It also highlighted disparities in patient access to communication and increased tensions around public perceptions, including frustration with the federal response and misinformation.

Working in the Pacific Northwest, we see a very active community with many trauma cases, especially in the summer. Harborview serves as the only level one trauma center in this region, which includes Washington, Alaska, Idaho, and Montana. The area’s outdoor lifestyle contributes to the types of injuries we treat regularly.

Harborview Medical Center serves patients not only from Washington but also from Alaska, as part of its four-state trauma service area. This means I sometimes care for trauma patients who have been transferred from Alaska to our facility. It highlights the broad regional responsibility we have as a level one trauma center.
cues: part of Harborview’s four-state service area

summary: Idaho is one of the four states, along with Washington, Alaska, and Montana, from which Harborview Medical Center receives trauma patients. This regional role means I care for critically injured patients coming from Idaho, reflecting the wide reach of our trauma services.
cues: part of Harborview’s four-state service area

summary: Montana is included in the four-state area served by Harborview Medical Center as the only level one trauma center. I have cared for trauma patients transferred from Montana, which shows the extensive geographic scope of our trauma services in the region.
cues: part of Harborview’s four-state service area

summary: The University of Washington has played a key role alongside Harborview in responding to the COVID-19 pandemic. Their coordinated efforts helped ensure we had access to proper PPE and safety protocols. The partnership between Harborview and UW has been crucial in managing the pandemic effectively in our region.

As an Asian-American nurse, I felt a deep frustration when I heard the term 'Chinese virus' used by the president during the pandemic. It was hurtful and contributed to a difficult social environment amid an already challenging time. This rhetoric affected not only me but also the broader community and complicated efforts to present a united front against COVID-19.
